behaviors.description: Migrations are applied in ascending numeric order determined by the filename prefix.
behaviors.justification: Real filesystem returns files in arbitrary order; mocks hide this. Real database needed to verify actual order of SQL execution. Cannot be tested without I/O.
behaviors.test_description: Set up fresh PostgreSQL database with migrations directory containing: 01.sql, 10.sql, 02.sql, 11.sql, 03.sql. Each creates a uniquely-named table (e.g., 01.sql→table_01). Run migration runner. Query _migrations table and verify entries in numeric order (01, 02, 03, 10, 11) and all tables exist.

behaviors.description: A migration that has already been applied is not re-executed on a subsequent run.
behaviors.justification: Mocked database checks hide bugs in transaction handling, commit logic, and idempotency queries. Real database with actual transaction semantics required to catch issues where check passes but side effects still occur.
behaviors.test_description: Create 2 migrations: 01.sql inserts 1 row into a table, 02.sql creates a new table. Run runner twice sequentially against same database. Verify: row count is still 1 (not 2) after second run, table created in 02.sql still exists, and _migrations has exactly 1 record per migration.

behaviors.description: When a migration statement fails, its changes are rolled back automatically and no further migrations in the same run are attempted.
behaviors.justification: Real PostgreSQL transaction semantics required. Mocked database cannot verify actual rollback behavior or ensure atomicity. Need to verify partial changes are cleaned up and subsequent migrations are skipped.
behaviors.test_description: Create 3 migrations: 01.sql (CREATE TABLE test_01), 02.sql (invalid SQL or constraint violation), 03.sql (CREATE TABLE test_03). Run against fresh database. Verify: test_01 table exists, test_03 table does NOT exist, no orphaned objects from 02.sql remain, _migrations table contains only 1 record (01).

behaviors.description: When two instances of the runner start at the same time against the same database, each migration is applied exactly once across both instances.
behaviors.justification: Database-level race conditions and locking failures only manifest with real separate OS processes connecting independently. In-process async tasks cannot trigger real PostgreSQL lock contention or primary key violations on concurrent inserts.
behaviors.test_description: Spawn 2 separate Node.js processes that simultaneously invoke the migration runner against the same PostgreSQL database with the same migration files. Use controlled timing or database synchronization primitives to force execution overlap. Verify: no primary key violation on _migrations, each migration appears exactly once, and all SQL side effects applied exactly once (e.g., INSERT produces 1 row, not 2).

behaviors.description: On the very first run against a fresh database, the _migrations table is created before any migration file is applied.
behaviors.justification: Real SQL execution required to verify DDL actually runs and schema is correct. Mocks cannot catch malformed CREATE TABLE statements, missing columns, or type mismatches. Real database state needed.
behaviors.test_description: Start with fresh PostgreSQL database (no _migrations table). Run migration runner with 2 migration files. Verify: _migrations table exists after first migration, has expected schema (name, applied_at columns), both migrations recorded in table, and running runner again does not error or attempt to recreate table.
